content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    KATHLEEN BROWN, Treasurer

OFFICE OF THE TREASURER
SACRAMENTO




                                        March 26, 1991




Blanca Breeze
Fair Political Practice Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA    95814

Dear Blanca:

     This is to request your advice about a question concerning
mass mailings by elected officials.  I would appreciate your
response in writing.

     At specific issue here is a program established by the State
Treasurer called the College Savings Bond Program.  Under
this program, we are trying to get citizens to buy certain
bonds which can be redeemed in the future to be used for
college expenses.

     To operate the program, we must advertise it in order to
"solicit" inquiries about the program from potential
investors.  The advertisements will only occur in general
circulation newspapers such as the Los Angeles Times.  Our
assumption is that only subscribers would receive the
newspaper, however, some copies may be given to schools
and/or other groups despite our intent.

     My specific question is "pursuant to the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act, what, if any,
restrictions would be placed on the content of these
advertisements?"

Sincerely,


Hal Geiogue

HAL GEIOQUE
Assistant Treasurer

sections.question: Pursuant to the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act, what, if any, restrictions would be placed on the content of these advertisements?
sections.question_synthetic: What restrictions, if any, does the Political Reform Act's mass mailing provisions place on advertisements for the State Treasurer's College Savings Bond Program?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or advice section is present. The question is clearly stated. Document appears to be from the Office of the Treasurer requesting guidance before the FPPC has responded.

I would appreciate your response in writing. At specific issue here is a program established by the State Treasurer called the College Savings Bond Program. Under this program, we are trying to get citizens to buy certain bonds which can be redeemed in the future to be used for college expenses. To operate the program, we must advertise it in order to "solicit" inquiries about the program from potential investors. The advertisements will only occur in general circulation newspapers such as the Los Angeles Times. Our assumption is that only subscribers would receive the newspaper, however, some copies may be given to schools and/or other groups despite our intent. My specific question is "pursuant to the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act, what, if any, restrictions would be placed on the content of these advertisements?" Sincerely, Hal Geiogue HAL GEIOQUE Assistant Treasurer
embedding.summary: Assistant Treasurer Hal Geiogue requests FPPC advice regarding mass mailing restrictions under the Political Reform Act as applied to advertisements for the State Treasurer's College Savings Bond Program to be published in general circulation newspapers.
